title: The Finished Work of Christ
intro: It is finished. Three words in English, one word in Greek — tetelestai. A word used in the ancient world to stamp a debt paid in full. When Jesus cried it from the cross, He was not announcing defeat. He was declaring completion.
reading:
Gavin's outline traces the finished work of Christ through four movements — His life, His death, His resurrection, and His ascension.

Isaiah 53:5-6 prophesied the death centuries before it happened: He was pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities. The punishment that brought us peace was on Him. Romans 5:8 declares that God demonstrates His love in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us — not after we cleaned up, not once we were worthy, while we were still far.

John 19:30 records the moment: it is finished. Everything required for our redemption had been accomplished. The record of debt — every sin, every charge — was canceled.

1 Corinthians 15:3-4 gives us the irreducible core: Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures, was buried, and was raised on the third day. Romans 4:25 tells us He was raised for our justification. The cross dealt with sin. The empty tomb conquered death itself.

Acts 1:9-11 records His ascension — taken up into heaven, a cloud receiving Him out of their sight. And Hebrews 7:25 tells us what He is doing now: He always lives to make intercession for those who draw near to God through Him.

The work is finished — and yet He is not passive. He is alive, at the right hand of the Father, interceding for you by name, right now. You do not add to it. You receive it.
reflection: Is there any area of your life where you are still trying to contribute to or earn what Christ has already fully accomplished? What would it mean to fully rest in a finished work?
abide: It is finished. I receive those words as the foundation of everything I am and everything I have. I do not come to You trying to complete what You have already completed. I come as one whose debt has been stamped — paid in full.
